questions.prompt: Your assistant's 'smart inbox cleanup' auto-archives unimportant emails 95% accurately. The 5% miss causes outsized angry feedback. Best response?

choices.text: Train a better classifier and keep auto-archiving.

Step the feature down to 'propose archives, user confirms' (level 3). 95% accuracy on a destructive action is below the trust bar — the misses generate disproportionate trust damage. Trust is asymmetric: a 5% miss rate spends much more trust than the 95% success rate builds. Level-3 keeps the value with no trust burn.

questions.explanation: Trust spending is asymmetric — surprises and mistakes cost more than wins earn. For destructive or hard-to-undo actions, even 95% accuracy can net negative trust. The right pattern: propose, user confirms. Same value, no surprises.

questions.prompt: What's the most reliable way to build a user's trust in an assistant over time?

choices.text: Transparency + asking before risky actions + consistency + quick undo + visible audit trail. Each successful, transparent interaction adds to the trust budget; surprises and hidden actions burn it. The pattern is durable: do small useful things well and visibly, then earn the right to do bigger things.

questions.explanation: Trust accrues from successful, transparent action — not from marketing or feature volume. Each interaction adds or subtracts; transparency multiplies. The best path to a level-5 autonomous future is a long string of level-3 wins where the user always knew what was happening.
